Subject:
NSI Daily Market Report 16/12/2025
Hi Deepesh
We are trading down across all benchmarks following losses in yesterday’s close.
In early Asian trade this morning oil prices had slipped as disappointing economic indicators have revealed that China\'s economy is stalling, reinforcing the downward pressure on crude benchmarks and weighing on market sentiment, whilst there\'s been rising optimism for the Russia-Ukraine peace talks. West Texas Intermediate and Brent crude have both been trading roughly 0.6% lower, reflecting the continued softness from the declines in the prior session. US officials have proposed NATO-style security guarantees for Ukraine during talks in Berlin, which Trump has suggested that the negotiators are "closer now than we have been ever". However, as optimism rises for a potential peace deal between Russia and Ukraine, pressure on markets also increase since a peace deal would likely lead to the sanctions on Russia to be lifted, resulting in Russian oil supply to flood the markets amid worries of a supply overhang. Notably, Trump has hyped up negotiations in several instances that most had ultimately failed to be finalised, therefore we can take the positive praise for the Russia-Ukraine peace  deal talks with a grain of salt until we know for certain which direction the negotiations are heading. Moreover, official statistics that had been released yesterday showed that China\'s factory output is slowing to a 15-month low and that retail sales are increasing at their slowest pace in nearly three years. These figures from the world’s largest crude importer have raised concerns over the strength of global oil demand in 2026, on top of the weak economic data out of China boosting concerns of an oversupply, causing the bearish narrative in oil markets to compound. Soft demand signals from China, coupled with ongoing trade and industrial headwinds in other parts of Asia, has resulted in macro pressures to be reinforced, which have played a large factor of keeping crude prices under strain for several months now. Until there is clear movement in demand signals or a major supply disruption, oil trader will continue to be reluctant over chasing higher prices.
On Monday, the European Union\'s Council had sanctioned five individuals and four entities that are responsible for supporting Russia’s shadow fleet and helping to move sanctioned oil. The sanctioned individuals are businessmen who are directly or indirectly linked to the recently sanctioned Rosneft and Lukoil, and are involved in an economic sector that provides substantial sources of revenues to Russia\'s government. The individuals are Murtaza Lakhani (a Canadian-Pakistani oil trader), Valery Kildiyarov (finance director of Lukoil’s Litasco Middle East DMCC), Anar Madatli, Talat Safarov, and Etibar Eyyub - a United Arab Emirates (UAE) based oil-trading company previously known as Coral Energy, which are shareholders and/or directors of 2Rivers Group. The EU had also commented in a press statement that the sanctioned individuals also “control vessels transporting crude oil or petroleum products, originating in Russia or being exported from Russia, concealing the actual origin of the oil, while practicing irregular and high-risk shipping practices”. The four entities that has been sanctioned are all shipping companies based in the UAE, Vietnam, and Russia, who either own or manage tankers that have been blacklisted for being part of Russia’s shadow fleet. The sanctioned entities are UAE-based Nova Shipmanagement LLC-FZ, Citrine Marine SPC, Vietnam-registered Hung Phat Maritime Trading, and Russia-based SeverTransBunker Company Limited. This new round of EU sanctions follows closely after the US implemented sanctions on Russia\'s two oil majors, which had disrupted the buying plans and trading patterns for most Indian refiners. Additionally, Russia\'s oil and gas revenues are set to be cut by almost 50% in December from a year earlier, dropping to the lowest level since August 2020, due to falling oil prices and strengthening Russian currency, according to Reuters calculations.
Biden had put restrictions on oil and gas exploration in Alaska, including the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge as well as federal lands in the state, before he left the office, but Trump has now removed the legislative protections. This follows after Trump administration had decided to reopen a larger portion of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuges for oil and gas drilling, which had been marked as the most aggressive push to expand exploration in far north Alaska since 2021, during Trump\'s first lease sale. In October, the Interior Department had claimed that it will restore the full 1.5-million-acre Coastal Plain to leasing, on top of reinstating the previously cancelled leases that\'s held by the Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority. The several congressional measures under the Congressional Review Act and catalysed a rapid reaction from the environmentalists, reported Bloomberg last week. The National Wildlife Federation had commented that the Trump\'s move was “a direct attack on public input, science, and responsible stewardship of public lands, wildlife, water, Indigenous communities, and rural economies,” the Brooks Range Coalition adding that the move “leaves Alaska’s rural communities, hunters, and Tribal governments with fewer protections at a time when climate change and resource pressures are rapidly intensifying.” Moreover, the Trump administration had removed restrictions and opened 82% of the National Petroleum Reserve for new drilling earlier this year. Notably, Alaska\'s oil production had peaked in 1988, its output reaching 2 million barrels per day. The nation continues to be one of the largest legacy oil-producing regions in the United states, but it currently only accounts for just 3% of US output; the decrease in production is mainly due to aging fields, high costs, and limited leasing, causing investments into its energy sector to be stalled for decades. However, this appears to be changing with the US now pursuing energy security, on top of an opportunity arising to boost its exports since Asian buyers are currently showing renewed interest in long-term crude and LNG supply.
